coredata.dc:title: A novel fusion-based deep learning approach with PSO and explainable AI for batteries State of Charge estimation in Electric Vehicles
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari, Sadiqa
Kim, Jisoo
Byun, Yung-Cheol
coredata.dc:description:
One of the main purposes of the Battery Management System (BMS) is to estimate the State of Charge (SoC) of Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). In this study, we propose a novel fusion model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Long Short-term Memory networks (LSTMs), and Convolutional LSTM (ConvLSTM) architectures to efficiently capture spatio-temporal patterns in battery data efficiently, hence improving the estimate accuracy of SoC. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is employed to optimize hyperparameters and enhance the model’s accuracy. The fusion model outperforms the separate CNN, LSTM, and ConvLSTM models regarding performance metrics. Specifically, the fusion model with PSO achieved a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.01, Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.094, and a
R
2
score of 99% according to experimental assessments. The results confirm the model’s effectiveness in estimating SoC, indicating its potential to enhance the dependability and efficiency of BMS. Furthermore, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) was employed to elucidate the battery SoC model’s decision-making process and identify the key elements contributing to the estimate process.

Battery
Deep learning
Battery management systems
Explainable Artificial Intelligence
Convolutional LSTM
Particle Swarm Optimization
Predictive modeling

head.ce:title: A novel fusion-based deep learning approach with PSO and explainable AI for batteries State of Charge estimation in Electric Vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
One of the main purposes of the Battery Management System (BMS) is to estimate the State of Charge (SoC) of Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). In this study, we propose a novel fusion model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Long Short-term Memory networks (LSTMs), and Convolutional LSTM (ConvLSTM) architectures to efficiently capture spatio-temporal patterns in battery data efficiently, hence improving the estimate accuracy of SoC. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is employed to optimize hyperparameters and enhance the model’s accuracy. The fusion model outperforms the separate CNN, LSTM, and ConvLSTM models regarding performance metrics. Specifically, the fusion model with PSO achieved a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.01, Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.094, and a
R
2
score of 99% according to experimental assessments. The results confirm the model’s effectiveness in estimating SoC, indicating its potential to enhance the dependability and efficiency of BMS. Furthermore, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) was employed to elucidate the battery SoC model’s decision-making process and identify the key elements contributing to the estimate process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy storage systems, often known as batteries, play a crucial role in current power technologies (Espedal et al., 2021; Jafari et al., 2023). Electric Vehicles (EVs) have the potential to contribute to the development of a sustainable mode of transport in the long term. This is because they produce minimal to zero carbon emissions, generate minimal noise, are highly efficient, and can easily integrate with grid management and connection systems (Ling et al., 2021). Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are highly ideal for powering EVs because of their continuous energy density and durability improvements. The Battery Management System (BMS) is a crucial component in EVs since it effectively monitors and makes informed decisions based on real-time data on the battery’s condition. Significant state characteristics often establish status knowledge of LIBs (Bian et al., 2022; Lombardo et al., 2021). However, the values of these state characteristics can only be inferred indirectly through quantifiable quantities like current, voltage, and temperature (Mao et al., 2022).

Hence, the precision of the models or algorithms employed to compute these state parameters is crucial in guaranteeing the proper functioning of the BMS. The SoC quantifies the remaining capacity of the lithium-ion battery, which is mostly influenced by the battery’s present electrical flow. An estimation can be performed using an Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) incorporating adaptive filtering techniques, such as the Kalman Filter (KF) (Andre et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2023). The ECM views the battery as a circuit encompassing various power supply elements, resistors, capacitors, and other factors (Saeed et al., 2023). Nevertheless, the process of parameterizing the ECM necessitates thorough battery testing to acquire precise SoC estimation (Tian et al., 2022). Recently, many Artificial Intelligence (AI) approaches have been proposed to determine the SoC of batteries. These tactics have demonstrated the capacity to surpass conventional procedures. The approaches utilize AI techniques to train by establishing correlations between cell assessment characteristics and the battery SoC estimate. This is accomplished by the examination of extensive data to detect and understand interconnectedness and recurring patterns. The AI model may be utilized to predict a battery’s SoC by applying it to an unknown dataset (Darbar and Bhattacharya, 2022). SoC estimate approaches can be categorized into Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), termed black-box models since they can establish a nonlinear link between quantifiable elements and the battery’s SoC. The Feedforward Neural Networks (FNNs) are commonly used for SoC estimation (Hannan et al., 2020), CNN (Yang et al., 2019), and LSTM networks (Chen et al., 2023). Vidal et al. (2020) have developed a stronger Back Propagation Neural Network (BPNN) that uses observed current, voltage, and temperature as input variables to forecast the SoC reliably. Although the BPNN method exhibited sluggish calculation time and was highly sensitive to the initial weight (Hannan et al., 2018), it could demonstrate gradient propagation or encounter the problem of falling into a local minimum. In order to address this problem, other scientists employed an artificial fish swarm approach to ascertain many optimum parameters for the BP neural network (Wang et al., 2020). However, applying the swarm intelligence optimization approach raised the computing expense when dealing with large data. Several AI methods failed to include the modeling of battery voltage. However, the SoC was represented as a sampled signal component. The SoC estimate was performed using several Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) with satisfactory accuracy, which, to determine the battery SoC, Chemali et al. (2017) has been suggested using an LSTM network.

Moreover, the current research emphasis has transitioned towards developing models that produce accurate predictions and demonstrate interpretability and comprehensibility. Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) facilitates the transformation of models that lack transparency into ones that are transparent by generating explanations. Models based on XAI exhibit comparable performance to human experts, enhancing their reliability. XAI provides a deeper understanding of the intricate reasoning embedded in the models, guaranteeing transparency (Linardatos et al., 2020; Holzinger et al., 2022; Jafari and Byun, 2024). Energy storage technologies, including LIBs, are crucial in contemporary EVs because of their exceptional energy density and efficiency. Precise assessment of the State of Charge (SoC) is crucial for maximizing battery efficiency and guaranteeing the safety and dependability of EVs. Conventional approaches for estimating SoC performance, such as ECM and KFs, frequently encounter non-linearities and need substantial parameterization, limiting the accuracy and effectiveness in dynamic situations. In this study, we provide a novel fusion-based Deep Learning (DL) model that combines the architectures of Convolutional LSTM (ConvLSTM), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The fusion model is enhanced using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), which can enhance accuracy and computational efficiency and render interpretable utilizing XAI approaches, notably SHAP values. Our methodology differs from traditional methods by utilizing CNNs to extract spatial features, LSTMs to capture temporal dynamics, and ConvLSTM to address spatio-temporal correlations, which the combination results in a thorough and accurate estimation of the SoC.
The main contributions of the suggested novel approach in this research study are summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: An integrated model is being developed to overcome the limitations of separate AI systems.

We suggest a hybrid model that amalgamates the advantageous features of CNNs, LSTMs, and ConvLSTMs. This integration enables the model to efficiently capture and evaluate intricate battery data’s spatial and temporal features.
Using PSO for effective hyperparameter selection results in enhanced predicted accuracy and computational efficiency.
Organizes a comprehensive comparison of traditional and cutting-edge SoC estimation techniques, showing the superiority of the suggested model.

The study thoroughly assesses the proposed model compared to individual implementations of CNN, LSTM, and ConvLSTM models. This comparison analysis illustrates the dominance of the fusion strategy, measured by many performance measures, including MAE, MSE, RMSE, and
R
2
.
Utilizing XAI techniques to guarantee transparency and instill confidence in the model’s predictions.
Evaluate and compare the proposed with current models.

ce:section.ce:para: The remaining paper is organized so that Section 2 explains a literature review, while Section 3 introduces the materials and methods. Section 4 discusses the dataset description and preprocessing, while Section 5 describes the detailed experimentation, analysis of results, and interpretation, followed by a conclusion and future directions in Section 6.

An investigation was conducted on the degradation characteristics of commercially available lithium-ion battery cells that utilize Nickel–Cobalt–Aluminum-oxide (NCA) electrodes when subjected to cyclic overcharging. Non-destructive approaches for detecting overcharging deterioration have been developed. The experiment results demonstrate a significant reduction in capacity with increasing overcharge depth and cycle count, particularly in the first three cycles and when the charging termination voltage approaches 5 V. The tolerance for overcharging has been reduced due to cyclic overcharging. Through the utilization of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, incremental capacity, and differential voltage analysis, three primary forms of degradation have been distinguished: a reduction in lithium has been supplied, a reduction in active materials, and a significant rise in the third peak of incremental capacity curves, which acts as a diagnostic characteristic for NCA battery degradation (Zhang et al., 2022). The EVs are crucial in mitigating emissions and addressing environmental concerns, but they face several technical challenges. The study has focused on the essential developments required in areas such as battery management, vehicle modeling, intelligent decision-making, and enhanced charging technologies, which are critical for developing EVs. The significance of infrastructure, standards, and regulatory frameworks in promoting the extensive use of EVs has also been highlighted. These conversations have offered a thorough examination of the present condition and future requirements in the advancement of EV technology (Xiong et al., 2022).

Due to the rapid progress in AI and Machine Learning (ML), there is an increasing need for traditional electronics to have the ability to think, analyze, and provide advice. This phenomenon has resulted in a heightened need for intelligent, self-sustaining technologies. A new study has investigated the application of nanogenerators in acoustics, emphasizing their potential when combined with AI and ML technologies. Nanogenerators are novel devices that extract electrical energy from external sources such as air vibrations or mechanical motions. The study has investigated many applications of nanogenerators in acoustics, including energy harvesting, sound detection, noise monitoring, and acoustic sensing. Moreover, research has demonstrated how AI and ML techniques can enhance the efficiency of nanogenerators. These approaches employ advanced data analysis and model training to optimize these devices for better acoustic applications (Yu et al., 2024).

Since battery packs with lithium-ion have so many advantages over other battery kinds – including longer lifespans, higher energy densities, and no memory effect – they are widely used in the EV industry (Watrin et al., 2012). In order to avoid a decrease in results and significant harm, a health management system is essential (Rivera-Barrera et al., 2017). Accurately predicting the lifespan of LIBs is essential for maximizing their utilization, upkeep, and economic evaluation in energy storage systems. An effective approach for this objective is the utilization of Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), renowned for its exceptional precision. In order to tackle this issue, in Liu et al. (2024), a new method has integrated Variational Auto-Encoders (VAE) with Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Units (BiGRU) to extract features automatically. It has estimated the lifespan of LIBs. The suggested approach has employed VAE to decrease the dimensionality of impedance data, which has resulted in a substantial reduction in computing complexity while retaining high-quality characteristics. BiGRU has utilized these properties to correlate the data with battery capacity, hence has improved the precision of the aging life calculation. The SoC estimate is crucial for accurately predicting driving range and preventing over- and over-discharging. This is essential for extending the lifespan of LIBs and guaranteeing the efficient functioning of EVs (Xing et al., 2014).

Thus, precise real-time measurements of the SoC are crucial to effectively maintaining battery health and providing a secure user experience with EVs. The SoC of a battery is chosen by separating its current capacity from its maximum capacity and then expressing it as a percentage. It can only be determined empirically by analyzing additional data, including voltage and current. Actual SoC readings are typically challenging to acquire due to several intricate external influences, particularly ambient temperature. A novel technique has been devised to precisely estimate the status of LIBs in EVs and battery energy storage systems using data-driven methods. The study examined the correlation between Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) and SoC battery estimation. This has been achieved by analyzing operational data gathered onboard, thereby avoiding the necessity for significant laboratory testing. By monitoring the changes in OCV about the amount of charge (ampere-hour or Ah) and implementing many reliability restrictions, this method has been developed to create OCV–SoC curves for the estimation of both SoC and SoH battery estimation (How et al., 2019). The estimation of SoC, a well-recognized technological constraint in EVs, has been thoroughly investigated through several methodologies, including the Coulomb-counting methodology, the OCV technique, model-based filter strategies, and the DL technique (Lu et al., 2013).

Eq. (1) calculates the SoC of a battery at a specific point in time T . The process starts with the starting SoC at time

C

, representing the charge used during this period. The OCV technique utilizes an OCV–SoC table in conjunction with the OCV measured by a sensor to determine a battery’s current SoC. Before using the OCV technique, it is necessary to temporarily halt the battery for a specific duration. Furthermore, ensuring the precise measurement of the open circuit voltage is crucial. This is particularly important in the SoC range, where the OCV–SoC curve of a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery remains constant. Therefore, even a minor discrepancy in voltage measurement might lead to a significant discrepancy in SoC calculation within the intermediate battery SoC range. To enhance the accuracy of evaluating the evolving characteristics of batteries in EVs, a group of researchers has merged the Coulomb-counting approach with model-based filter strategies to estimate the SoC battery (Plett, 2004).

The filter method relies on a prediction–correction process, enabling it to mitigate unexpected disruptions effectively. This method of SoC estimation utilizes the observed voltage as the state variable and compares it to the estimated voltage. The discovered error is utilized to modify the state using a suitable gain. Several model-based filtering algorithms have been created, each utilizing distinct gain computations. These include the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), the Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF), the Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF), and the Particle Filter (PF) (Wang et al., 2021). A fractional second-order model has been used in the study to offer a co-estimation technique for the SoC and SoH of EV batteries. The model parameters were determined using an adaptive evolutionary algorithm, and the SoC was calculated using a Multi-Innovations Unscented Kalman Filter (MIUKF). The UKF has been utilized to anticipate the SoH and update the capacity of the SoC estimator. The approach has surpassed classic UKF and other fractional-order KF in terms of performance and has shown excellent flexibility and accuracy (Ma et al., 2022).
Table 1 displays a diverse range of SoC estimate methods for batteries. Battery SoC estimate techniques can be organized into definition-based, model-based, and data-driven approaches.

Neural networks and ML are the primary components of data-driven models. ML is capable of processing and analyzing complex, nonlinear problems using enormous amounts of high-dimensional data. Consequently, several researchers have carried out investigations on battery SoC estimations using ML methodologies. In Zhao et al. (2022) have introduced an extreme learning machine approach for estimating the SoC of batteries. This progress involved constructing an ECM to accomplish the SoC estimate. Hai et al. (2023) employed deep neural networks to predict SoC for battery systems, specifically focusing on two typical driving cycle circumstances. The study also assessed the influence of several parameters on the precision of battery SoC prediction. The results indicate that a vehicle’s driving behavior and acceleration substantially influence the accuracy of battery SoC predictions. In Ren et al. (2021) has shown that modifying the hyperparameters of an active PSO-LSTM network can greatly enhance its accuracy. It is necessary to assess DL techniques following optimization to get improved performance. Bian et al. (2020) have evaluated the ability of multi-layer bidirectional LSTM, Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and standard RNN to estimate SoC. Their outcomes demonstrated that the multi-layer bidirectional LSTM achieved superior prediction accuracy compared to the other models, emphasizing the significance of optimizing the NN topology before training. Furthermore, GRU techniques were recognized for their computational efficiency, attaining comparable precision to LSTM while employing a less complex gating mechanism. By combining a CNN with LSTM or GRU, Song et al. (2019), Huang et al. (2019) improved the estimate of the during transient test cycles in the battery SoC.

This method produced more accurate results than using the CNN, LSTM, or GRU separately. This approach yielded higher accuracy than individually utilizing CNN, LSTM, or GRU. In addition, the Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN), which combines the benefits of CNN and RNN, demonstrated superior accuracy compared to networks that utilize LSTM or GRU. Nevertheless, combining TCN with other DL techniques to enhance performance is recommended, as notable inaccuracies in estimating SoC persist. The experiments demonstrate that combining CNN or TCN with LSTM or GRU improves the accuracy of SoC predictions. However, considerable variations and transitory errors in SoC calculations can occur due to massive transient voltage and current data oscillations. The study has introduced a process for estimating the SoH of EV batteries utilizing Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA). The technique has successfully solved issues in real-world SoH metering by utilizing comparable IC-value estimates for battery packs, doing cell-level checks, and accounting for cell inconsistency. Based on ICA, the suggested methodology has allowed for accurate prediction of SoH at the pack level by utilizing cell-level testing (She et al., 2021).

This section presents a proposed study for our work, providing a comprehensive explanation of the methodology used to determine the SoC of batteries in EVs. First, we describe theoretical foundation of the proposed model then we explain the LSTM network. Next, we describe the ConvLSTM architecture and the integration of CNN with LSTM (CNN–LSTM). In addition, we elucidate the optimization procedure utilizing PSO and the use of XAI approaches to augment the interpretability and reliability of the model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical foundation of the proposed model
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we present a complex deep-learning framework that improves the accuracy of SoC battery prediction. The architecture is depicted in Fig. 1. The suggested model, with its precision, utilizes the collective capabilities of CNNs, LSTMs, and ConvLSTM networks to predict the SoC in batteries. CNNs are specially designed to use several basic components, including convolution, pooling, and fully connected layers, to automatically and flexibly learn spatial hierarchies of properties through backpropagation. CNNs are utilized in SoC estimation to extract spatial characteristics from battery data, capturing detailed patterns and structures essential for precise predictions. LSTMs, RNNs, can learn complex relationships over extended periods and retain information about past states in sequential data. LSTMs are highly effective for time-series data, as they capture the crucial link between present and historical observations. Our model utilizes LSTM units to effectively record the sequential patterns in the battery’s charge–discharge cycles. This allows the model to acquire knowledge from past data and accurately predict future states.

ConvLSTMs enhance LSTMs by integrating convolution operations into the LSTM structure. They can simultaneously capture spatial and temporal connections, making them well-suited for challenges involving spatiotemporal prediction. Our model utilizes ConvLSTM layers to analyze the spatiotemporal data of battery operations. These layers integrate the temporal sequence learning capabilities of LSTMs with the spatial feature extraction capabilities of CNNs. The proposed model efficiently learns and interprets the complicated spatiotemporal patterns in battery data by including CNNs, LSTMs, and ConvLSTMs. This hybrid methodology guarantees that the model can effectively capture the complex interconnections in the data, resulting in more precise and dependable battery SoC predictions. In order to improve the model, we employed PSO to adjust the hyperparameters finely, boosting the model’s performance in different settings. The model underwent training and validation using specific datasets, with continuous validation to prevent overfitting. The performance evaluation was carried out utilizing measures such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and R-squared (
R
2
). We evaluated our hybrid model by comparing it against separate CNN, LSTM, and ConvLSTM approaches and the most advanced SoC estimate techniques available. Explainable AI (XAI) approaches, notably Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), were used to interpret the algorithm’s predictions to provide transparency and a clear understanding of the value of features in battery SoC estimation.

RNNs (Ullah et al., 2021) often encounter several obstacles, such as the vanishing gradient problem and limits in short-term memory, which become more noticeable when dealing with lengthy sequences. These challenges result from the inherent characteristics of backpropagation across time, wherein gradients can diminish significantly, resulting in inefficient learning in the early layers of the network (Bengio et al., 1994). As a result, RNNs face difficulties retaining crucial information from the early stages of the input when dealing with extended sequences. The gradient updates the weights during backpropagation; however, if the gradient decreases too much, the early layers get insufficient updates and cease to function as learning machines. In order to tackle these problems, other iterations of RNNs have been created, including the LSTM network. This particular network, introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997), is highly regarded and extensively employed. LSTMs are specifically engineered to address the vanishing gradient problem by integrating memory cells that can retain information for extended durations.

The cells employ three distinct kinds of gates, namely input, output, and forget gates, to regulate the transmission of information. This enables the network to manage and modify cell states effectively. The mathematical model of an LSTM unit is described in Eqs. (2),(3),(4), (5), (6), (7):

The sigmoid function is represented by σ , while the hyperbolic tangent function is indicated by tanh . The input at a specific time step t is described by

LSTMs effectively capture long associations in sequential data, completing them appropriate for applications operating time-series data and sequences to estimate the SoC for EV batteries.

Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) is a sophisticated deep-learning method to tackle space–time sequence estimation issues. These problems involve data sequences with spatial and temporal dimensions as input and output. ConvLSTM networks excel in capturing complex space–time patterns in datasets with nonlinear features, making them well-suited for applications that involve analyzing spatiotemporal data (Lee and Kim, 2020). The ConvLSTM methodology’s architectural overview is shown in Fig. 2.

The ConvLSTM network enhances the conventional LSTM by integrating convolutional structures into the LSTM’s gates, enabling it to analyze and forecast spatiotemporal data efficiently. The ConvLSTM architecture for time series analysis can be mathematically formulated as shown in Eqs. (8), (9),(10), (11), (12), (13):

The CNN–LSTM model is a hybrid architecture that combines the characteristics of CNNs and LSTM networks (Jafari and Byun, 2023a; Safavi et al., 2024). This architectural design is especially advantageous for tasks involving the extraction of spatial data and the acquisition of temporal sequences. The CNN layers of a CNN–LSTM model are employed to extract spatial information from the input data. The spatial characteristics are inputted into LSTM layers, effectively capturing the data’s temporal relationships. This combination enables the model to process and interpret complicated spatio-temporal sequences efficiently. The CNN component of the model often comprises convolutional and pooling layers. The convolutional layers perform convolution operations on the input data, detecting and acquiring spatial characteristics, while the pooling layers decrease the dimensionality of the data, retaining the most significant features. Mathematically, the processes performed by the CNN layers can be expressed in the following manner in Eq. (14):
(14)
Q = W ∗ x + b

Eq. (14) illustrates the convolution operation, in which Q describes the feature map, W represents the filter, x describes the input data, and b represents the bias term. The activation function, as seen in Eq. (15):
(15)

denotes the pooling operation that is applied to the active feature map. The CNN layers produce a collection of spatial characteristics, which are reconfigured and inputted into the LSTM layers. The LSTM module of the model analyzes these characteristics over time to understand sequential relationships, employing the identical LSTM formulas mentioned before. Eq. (17), and (18) provide the following description of how CNN and LSTM are integrated into the CNN–LSTM model:
(17)

refers to the hidden state from the previous time step. The CNN–LSTM model combines CNNs’ ability to extract spatial features with LSTM networks’ ability to learn temporal sequences.

Our work utilizes a fusion approach called CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM to improve the accuracy of estimating the SoC in electric car batteries. This technique combines the ability of CNNs to extract spatial features with the strong temporal data processing capabilities of LSTMs and ConvLSTM networks. At first, CNN layers examine the input data to detect spatial relationships and patterns, such as changes in voltage and current, that are crucial for the first calculation of SoC. The CNNs extract features, which are then inputted into LSTM layers. These LSTM layers capture the temporal sequences and dynamics, which are essential for comprehending the charge–discharge cycles of the battery as they unfold over time. The ConvLSTM layer combines the ability to handle spatial and temporal data simultaneously, thoroughly studying the battery’s activity. This fusion method guarantees a reliable estimation of SoC by capturing complex patterns and interconnections in the data frequently overlooked by individual model techniques.

This part presents the PSO algorithm created by Eberhart and Kennedy in 1995. It is specifically designed to optimize nonlinear functions (Kennedy and Eberhart, 1995). PSO is developed by drawing inspiration from natural occurrences like bird flocking and schooling in nature, which exhibit swarm behavior. The PSO algorithm has been widely used in several domains because of its strong resilience, straightforward implementation, and capacity to explore global solutions (Anton et al., 2015). The algorithm is a meta-heuristic optimization strategy that relies on a population of swarm particles to iteratively search for the optimal position with the best values. The swarm population attains the optimal value at two distinct sites, namely the local and global best. During an iterative process, the term local highest refers to the most favorable position inside the current iteration, while global best represents the second-best location for the ideal value over all previous iterations. The viewpoint of PSO is derived from the determination of the swarm’s position and speed. The approach aims to find the best possible outcomes by optimizing the swarm’s location and velocity as it moves across the search area. The goal value is achieved by minimizing the objective functions using an iterative process. This study implements the PSO algorithm to acquire the ideal hyperparameter values, such as the activation function, loss function, and learning rate. Fig. 3 displays the schematic of the PSO used for the suggested SoC battery prediction approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Explainable AI using SHAP for model interpretation
ce:section.ce:para:
The XAI improves the clarity of ML and Deep Learning (DL) models by clarifying the process of decision-making. First, a model is trained by using input characteristics to generate outputs. While the model can predict outcomes using fresh inputs, comprehending the impact of specific characteristics on these forecasts is complex. In the context of SoC prediction, a model employs inputs such as voltage and current to predict the SoC. Although, the importance of each input is only evident if XAI techniques are employed. SHAP values are calculated after the model has been trained and are utilized to examine the outcomes of the predictions. This study assists in identifying the crucial characteristics that influence the battery SoC predictions, confirming the algorithm’s efficacy and bolstering trust in its predictive capability. The utilization of SHAP not only elucidates the individual impact of each feature but also reinforces the general trustworthiness of the model by showcasing that its predictions are derived from important data insights rather than random data correlations (Zhang et al., 2020; Lundberg and Lee, 2017).

Eq. (19) computes the mean border assistance of each characteristic i across all possible hybrids, offering a logical justification for each prediction.

The suggested model’s performance is assessed by operating four battery datasets, including battery005, battery006, battery007, and battery018 from battery NASA dataset. The datasets comprise three distinct operating profiles: charging, discharging, and impedance. All of these profiles were conducted under ambient conditions. All of these profiles were completed at room temperature (Saha and Goebel, 2007). Table 2 presents the essential specifications of the LIBs utilized, encompassing their designated voltage, capacity, and weight.

The batteries were charged using the Constant Current (CC) and Constant Voltage (CV) technique, with a steady current of 1.5 A until the voltage reached 4.2 V. Afterwards, a CV was provided until the current decreased to 20 mA. Additionally, the discharge profile occurs at a consistent current of 2 A until the cell voltage drops to 2.7, 2.5, 2.2, and 2.5 V for each battery. The dataset includes an analysis of the impedance profile and discharge profile, which the comprehensive information on the properties of the batteries as shown in Table 3.
Fig. 4 illustrates the capacity deterioration curve for several batteries when subjected to continuous charging and discharging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample data from the charging and discharging profile
ce:section.ce:para: Within the charging and discharging operation, there is a constant movement of lithium ions into and out of the particles that compose the electrode. The erratic dispersion of lithium ions influences the lifespan of the battery. Increased dispersion and unequal distribution of lithium ions directly impact battery particles, resulting in reduced battery lifespan. Therefore, it is crucial to comprehend the attributes of the charging and discharging patterns for different battery parameters. The study reveals that the current during the discharging process exhibits significant irregularities over time, making it challenging to determine internal parameters. In contrast, obtaining internal parameters in the charging profile is comparatively easy due to using pre-set procedures. Consequently, the data is obtained from the charging profiles to record the variations in internal battery properties (Park et al., 2020). Fig. 5 depicts the charging efficiency of battery B0005. Figure (a) displays the temporal voltage profile, illustrating the voltage reaching 4.2 V and remaining constant as the current steadily diminishes. Subfigure (b) illustrates the current profile, which begins at a steady current of 1.5 A and gradually decreases to 20 mA while maintaining a voltage of 4.2 V. Figure (c) displays the temperature profile as the battery is being charged, illustrating the variations in temperature during the charging process.

Fig. 6 depicts the discharge characteristics of battery005. Figure (a) displays the temporal voltage profile, illustrating the gradual reduction in voltage as the battery undergoes discharge. Subfigure (b) illustrates the current profile, which begins with a consistent current of 2 A and remains at this level until the discharging process is complete. The temperature profile during discharging is depicted in subfigure (c), illustrating the variations in temperature as the battery flows through the discharge process.

In order to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the suggested model for predicting the SoC in LIBs, a thorough data pretreatment pipeline was executed on the NASA battery dataset using tools like Python and libraries such as Pandas and Scikit-learn. The procedure involves many crucial steps: managing missing values, standardizing the dataset, extracting and engineering features, implementing data augmentation techniques, and dividing the data for model training and assessment.

The initial stage entailed a comprehensive dataset analysis to detect and resolve any missing values. Linear interpolation was employed to estimate missing values in tiny gaps by analyzing the patterns in neighboring data points, maintaining the dataset’s continuity. If significant gaps were found, the impacted data segments were removed to preserve the dataset’s integrity and trustworthiness, which ensured that only full data were used in the modeling process. After handling missing data, the dataset was normalized using Min–Max scaling. This approach standardized all characteristics, including voltage, current, temperature, and impedance, to a uniform range from 0 to 1. Normalization played a critical role in equalizing the impact of features during the training stage, hence avoiding any distortions in the model’s learning process caused by differences in feature magnitudes.

During this stage, crucial characteristics related to battery performance were isolated, specifically emphasizing voltage, current, temperature, and charging/discharging cycles. In addition to these fundamental measurements, other characteristics were created to capture more intricate elements of battery performance. For example, we computed the voltage and current change rate, which offers a valuable understanding of how the battery reacts dynamically in various situations. The extensive range of features improves the model’s capacity to understand complex patterns related to SoC batteries, hence enhancing the model’s prediction accuracy and resilience.

In order to improve the strength and ability to apply to different situations of the hybrid DL model, we suggested that we utilize certain methods to increase the amount of data and adapt it to the specific features of the NASA battery dataset. These strategies were crucial for surpassing the constraints of a very limited dataset and for emulating a broader spectrum of operating circumstances. The main technique employed was random oversampling, which aimed to rectify the dataset imbalance by augmenting the number of underrepresented battery cycles. This strategy facilitated the model’s successful learning under various operating situations. In addition, we created artificial data using the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE). SMOTE generates synthetic samples by interpolating existing data points, specifically emphasizing minority class instances. This process enhances the coverage of the feature space and improves the model’s ability to make generalizations.

Following preprocessing, the dataset was partitioned into training, testing, and validation subsets according to a conventional distribution of 70% for training, 20% for testing, and 10% for validation. The segmentation, outlined in Table 4, was crucial for ensuring efficient model training and fair performance assessment. The training set was utilized to learn and optimize the model, while the validation set was employed to fine-tune the hyperparameters. Finally, the testing set assessed the model’s prediction abilities.

We utilized PSO to optimize the CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM model, which Table 5 presents a comprehensive overview of the optimized hyperparameters, the range of values examined throughout the search, and the optimal values obtained using PSO.

The PSO method was used to reduce the validation loss, with MSE as the main evaluation measure. This procedure guaranteed that the model reached its best possible setup, finding the right balance between several hyperparameters to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of predicting the battery condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of results, interpretation, and discussion
ce:section.ce:para:
This section showcases the assessment outcomes of the suggested model for estimating the SoC and examines the significance of these findings. The performance of the model is assessed using many metrics, including MAE, MSE, RMSE, and
R
2
. In addition, the findings are compared with various baseline models to emphasize the enhancements gained by the suggested method and the utilization of PSO.

A comprehensive analysis of the experimental setup is essential for understanding the technological foundation upon which our suggested strategy developed and evaluated. The specifics of this setup are delineated in Table 6, which provides a summary of the fundamental technologies and equipment employed in our inquiry. The technical infrastructure meticulously designed to fulfill the precise needs of our ML workflow, encompassing data preparation, model training, optimization, and assessment. The chosen operating system and CPU specifications accurately offer the necessary computing capacity and efficiency to handle large datasets and complex model architectures, including ConvLSTM. The primary programming language and its associated deep-learning framework facilitated the development of sophisticated models. Simultaneously, optimizing and explainable AI tools facilitated the modification of model parameters and enhanced comprehension of the models’ decision-making process.

The suggested model is evaluated using four essential error metrics such as MAE, RMSE, MSE, and
R
2
(Jafari and Byun, 2023b). These measurements offer a thorough evaluation of the model’s precision and dependability. MAE quantifies the normalized size of the discrepancies between the projected and real battery SoC values. It measures the average prediction error and is less influenced by extreme values than MSE and RMSE. The MSE is a mathematical metric that computes the average of the squared differences between the predicted and real SoC values for the battery. This metric is particularly sensitive to greater mistakes since it squares discrepancies. RMSE is a metric that quantifies the size of prediction errors in the same units as the data. It is derived from the MSE by taking the square root. The RMSE measures prediction errors that are easy to read. The
R
2
quantifies the amount of variance in the real SoC that the model can accurately predict. Higher values of
R
2
imply greater predictive accuracy. Extensive experiments were conducted utilizing various DL models, such as LSTM, ConvLSTM, CNN–LSTM, and CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM, with and without PSO. The following equations define the metrics utilized in this study (20), (21), (22), (23):

Computational efficiency is crucial when implementing ML models, especially in real-time applications such as BMS. The training and testing timeframes for each SoC prediction model assessed in this work are presented in Table 7. The training time is the time necessary to train the model using the given dataset, while the testing time denotes the length needed to make predictions using the trained model.

Based on the data, it is clear that the CNN–LSTM model has the shortest training time, taking just 297 s. The CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM model comes in second place with a training time of 359 s, which is quite close. The efficiency of the CNN–LSTM architecture is due to its lower complexity compared to the LSTM and ConvLSTM models. The latter models require longer sequences to capture temporal relationships effectively. Nevertheless, the ConvLSTM model, despite its longer training duration, exhibits a comparatively efficient testing duration, suggesting a favorable trade-off between model intricacy and inference speed. While more computationally intensive during training than the CNN–LSTM model, the CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM model provides a well-balanced compromise with testing durations similar to those of the CNN–LSTM model. The LSTM model has the longest testing period in addition to the longest training period, indicating that its more intricate temporal dependence capturing method requires significant computational capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results without and with PSO optimization
ce:section.ce:para: This subsection analyzes the efficacy of PSO optimization on several models by comparing their performance metrics and prediction accuracy with and without PSO. The findings unequivocally indicate a substantial enhancement in the predictive accuracy of each model following the use of PSO optimization. This subsection examines the efficacy of PSO optimization on several models by comparing their performance metrics and prediction accuracy with and without PSO. The findings unequivocally indicate a substantial enhancement in the predictive accuracy of each model following the use of PSO optimization.

The influence of PSO on the model’s performance was evaluated by comparing the training and validation losses over the epochs. Fig. 7 displays the loss curves for the model without PSO optimization in 7(a) and with PSO optimization in 7(b). The data clearly illustrates that utilizing PSO greatly enhances the speed at which convergence occurs and decreases the ultimate loss values for both the training and validation datasets.

Figs. 8, 9, 10 depict the performance of several models in predicting the SoC, which illustrates the outcomes of the LSTM, ConvLSTM, and CNN–LSTM methods before applying the PSO algorithm and after applying PSO algorithm. The actual SoC values are compared to the anticipated values in each figure. The findings unequivocally indicate that the implementation of PSO greatly enhances the predictive precision for all models.

Fig. 8 depicts the comparison of the LSTM model’s accuracy in estimating the SoC of a battery, with and without the implementation of PSO. Fig. 8(a) displays the LSTM model without PSO, revealing noticeable discrepancies from the actual SoC values, suggesting less precise forecasts. Fig. 8(b) illustrates the enhanced performance of the LSTM model when optimized using PSO. The predictions nearly match the actual SoC values, indicating the efficiency of PSO in improving the model’s accuracy.

Fig. 9 depicts the contrast between the actual SoC values and the anticipated SoC values obtained from the ConvLSTM model, both with and without PSO. Fig. 9(a) illustrates that the ConvLSTM model without PSO displays more discrepancies, indicating less accuracy predictions, which 9(b) demonstrates that the ConvLSTM model, optimized using PSO, has a higher degree of agreement between the actual and anticipated SoC values, suggesting enhanced prediction accuracy.

Fig. 10 shows how the CNN–LSTM model, with and without PSO optimization compares the predicted and real SoC values. Fig. 10(a) demonstrates that the model lacking PSO exhibits notable variations and deviates from the genuine SoC values, suggesting less precise predictions. In contrast, Fig. 10(b) illustrates that the model improved with PSO accurately follows the actual SoC values, highlighting the efficacy of PSO in improving the prediction accuracy of the CNN–LSTM model.

Fig. 11 compares the prediction performance of the suggested CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM method for SoC, both with and without PSO. Fig. 11, which (a) illustrates the SoC prediction outcomes of the suggested technique without the use of PSO, whereas (b) demonstrates the SoC prediction performance with PSO. The real SoC values are placed alongside the anticipated values in each subfigure to facilitate a direct comparison. The outcomes unequivocally show that PSO improves prediction accuracy. The estimated SoC values in 11(b) exhibit a significantly higher degree of alignment with the actual values in comparison to 11(a), suggesting that the model’s performance is greatly enhanced by PSO optimization. The enhancement shows the efficacy of PSO in optimizing the model parameters, resulting in more accurate and dependable SoC predictions.

Table 8 summarizes the performance of several techniques for SoC prediction in the absence of PSO. Table 8 displays the assessment measures, including the MAE, MSE, RMSE, and the
R
2
. The outcomes display that the suggested fusion technique performs better than the individual LSTM, ConvLSTM, and CNN–LSTM models in all criteria. More precisely, the presented approach attains the minimum MAE of 0.05, MSE of 0.04, and RMSE of 0.2, in addition to the greatest
R
2
value of 93%. The findings demonstrate that the fusion technique effectively captures the intricate spatio-temporal patterns in the battery data, resulting in more accurate and dependable SoC battery predictions.

Table 9 displays the performance metrics of several models for estimating SoC with the incorporation of PSO. The findings demonstrate a notable increase in the efficacy of all models when improved using PSO. The CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM–PSO approach obtains the lowest MAE of 0.01, MSE of 0.009, and RMSE of 0.094. Additionally, it achieves the greatest
R
2
value of 99%. This study showcases the efficacy of integrating the fusion approach with PSO to improve the precision and dependability of SoC predictions.

Fig. 12 illustrates the performance of several models in predicting the SoC. This includes the suggested technique, both with and without PSO. Fig. 12(a) displays the performance without PSO, where the suggested technique already exhibits superior alignment with the genuine SoC values compared to other methods. Fig. 12(b) demonstrates the performance of PSO, emphasizing the substantial increase in prediction accuracy for all models. The proposed approach stands out for its exceptional accuracy, as its predictions nearly align with the actual SoC values.

The results in Fig. 12 indicate that incorporating PSO greatly improves the predictive accuracy of all models. In 12(a), in the absence of PSO, there are significant disparities between the actual SoC values and the predictions made by all approaches, especially the LSTM and ConvLSTM models. The suggested approach exhibits comparatively superior performance, while enhancement potential exists. On the other hand, 12(b) demonstrates that using PSO results in a significantly improved alignment between the shown SoC values from all models and the real values. The CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM–PSO approach, specifically, demonstrates the highest level of accuracy, as its predictions closely align with the actual SoC values. This demonstrates the notable increase in model performance due to PSO, illustrating its efficacy in optimizing model parameters to attain more accurate and dependable SoC predictions.

Figs. 13, 14, 15, and 16 show the outcomes, which offer a thorough assessment of the performance metrics for a number of models with and without PSO. These results demonstrate the notable enhancements in model efficacy attributed to PSO. For example, the comparison of MSE in Fig. 13 clearly shows a significant decrease in error for all models when PSO is used. The suggested CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM model achieves the lowest MSE. Fig. 14 demonstrates that the MAE significantly decreases following PSO optimization, highlighting the improved accuracy of the proposed technique. The proposed method obtains the lowest MAE compared to other models. The RMSE, as seen in Fig. 15, demonstrates substantial enhancement when utilizing PSO, hence confirming the model’s accuracy in predicting the battery SoC. Finally, Fig. 16 compares the
R
2
scores, which clearly show that PSO significantly enhances the accuracy of the models in predicting SoC, with the suggested technique achieving the greatest
R
2
score. These results demonstrate the significant advantages of integrating PSO into the model optimization process. The proposed CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM method consistently outperforms other models in all metrics, proving its effectiveness in providing accurate and dependable SoC predictions.

Table 10 displayed a comprehensive comparison of our proposed method-PSO with various cutting-edge techniques for estimating SoC in batteries. The comparison centers around two primary metrics: MAE and RMSE.

Our suggested method demonstrates superior performance compared to current strategies, obtaining the lowest MAE of 0.01 and RMSE of 0.1. This emphasizes the exceptional precision and resilience of our method. For instance, conventional deep learning models such as the Deep Neural Network (DNN) and the Feedforward Deep Neural Network (FDNN) exhibit greater MAE values of 1.413 and 0.09, respectively. In contrast, our approach displays significantly fewer errors, suggesting superior predictive ability. Our suggested model outperforms hybrid approaches like the TSO-BP-EKF, which combines optimization algorithms with KF regarding error margins. The outcomes clearly show that the CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM–PSO method effectively improves the accuracy and reliability of the battery SoC estimate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interpretation of feature importance for the SoC battery outcomes
ce:section.ce:para: Interpreting the model’s predictions is essential to guarantee transparency and dependability after assessing the results of the suggested approach. Explainable AI approaches, such as SHAP, are utilized to interpret the model and ascertain the importance of each feature in estimating the SoC battery.

The SHAP value plots for the suggested method, displayed in Fig. 17, demonstrate the effect of various factors on the prediction of battery SoC. The first figure (a) displays each feature’s mean absolute SHAP importance, which indicates their overall significance in the model’s predictions. The second panel (b) displays the supply of SHAP metrics for each feature, providing insights into how different feature values affect the model’s outcome. The analysis indicates that the SoC estimation is most significantly influenced by features such as battery voltage, current, and capacity.

Fig. 18 depicts the average SHAP values, which indicate the significance of several variables in predicting the SoC for EV batteries. The studied features encompass battery capacity, voltage measurement, terminal voltage, current measurement, terminal current, cycle count, battery temperature, and time. The SHAP values are compared across four different models: the proposed hybrid CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM model optimized utilizing PSO, LSTM optimized with PSO, ConvLSTM optimized with PSO, and CNN–LSTM optimized with PSO.

Fig. 18 illustrates the varying importance that each model places on different characteristics. The suggested technique prioritizes, especially regarding battery capacity and terminal voltage. This suggests sophisticated knowledge and responsiveness to these aspects when estimating the SoC. This research shows that the suggested model successfully captures important factors affecting the SoC and the model’s capacity to provide a clear understanding of the decision-making process in the battery management system.

In order to increase the SoC estimate accuracy for BMS, we suggested in this study a novel fusion CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM neural network supplemented with Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). The primary contributions are: (1) CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM has robust capabilities in modeling and analyzing time series data. (2) By integrating CNN–LSTM–ConvLSTM with PSO, the PSO method is employed to optimize the hyperparameters. According to experimental data, the suggested fusion neural network-based PSO offers an effective estimate of the SoC, exhibiting remarkable performance and a
R
2
score of 99%. The suggested model achieves the most accurate SoC estimate compared to existing techniques, such as standalone LSTM, ConvLSTM, and CNN–LSTM models, with an MAE of 0.01, MSE of 0.009, and RMSE of 0.094. The outcomes of several data sets demonstrate that the suggested model surpasses LSTM, CNN–LSTM, and ConvLSTM. The PSO algorithm played a crucial role in refining the model’s hyperparameters, resulting in a substantial improvement in both prediction accuracy and efficiency. Moreover, XAI methods, namely SHAP, offered crucial insights into the contributions of individual features to the model’s predictions. This enhanced transparency and dependability, guaranteeing that the model’s selections are grounded on a comprehensive comprehension of relevant aspects.

In order to guarantee scalability and effective data management, our future study will look at optimization strategies other than PSO and evaluate the model’s performance using larger datasets. Our objective is to improve the model by integrating other battery chemistries, such as Nickel–Cobalt–Aluminum (NCA) and lithium iron phosphate (LFP), to get accurate outcomes for various types of batteries. Furthermore, we want to assess the model’s performance over a broader temperature range, including below-freezing temperatures and different SoH. This will need training on datasets covering various environmental parameters and battery health situations. In order to satisfy the requirement for a more extensive range of experimental data and varied loading circumstances, further investigations will involve subjecting the model to dynamic loading conditions such as the Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS) and the Dynamic Stress Test (DST). By doing this, the model can function effectively in various real-life situations and different operating settings. Additional enhancements will consider characteristics such as battery age, usage habits, and other environmental conditions to offer more accurate evaluations of the battery SoC. We aim to utilize the model in real-time applications to validate its practical efficacy and explore sophisticated XAI techniques to understand the model’s decision-making mechanisms better.

Sadiqa Jafari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jisoo Kim: Software, Resources. Yung-Cheol Byun: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.
